required.title: [Equipment] MIG welder (110V or 220V), suitable for mild steel, with shielding gas (75/25 argon/CO2)
[Equipment] Angle grinder (4.5-inch) with grinding discs, cutting discs, and flap discs
[Tools] Hacksaw with bi-metal blades, metal files, C-clamps, welding magnets, speed square, soapstone or metal-marking pencil, tape measure, combination square
[PPE] Auto-darkening welding helmet (shade 10-13), safety glasses (worn under the helmet), leather welding gloves, leather apron or welding jacket, steel-toed boots, hearing protection
[Materials] Mild steel flat bar (1/8" and 3/16" x 1" and 2"), angle iron (1" x 1" x 1/8"), square tubing (1" x 1" x 1/16"), steel plate (1/4" for practice coupons)

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: Essential Craftsman — welding and fabrication series

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] AWS D1.1 Structural Welding Code

competence_markers.description: The student can cut metal to dimension with a hacksaw and an angle grinder, maintaining accuracy within 1/16"
The student can operate a MIG welder safely and produce consistent beads on flat plate
The student can weld butt joints, T-joints, and lap joints in flat position with acceptable quality (consistent width, fusion at edges, no visible porosity or cold lap)
The student can grind and finish welds to a clean, professional appearance
The student has designed, cut, fitted, welded, and finished at least two fabricated projects, including one that bears load
The student can evaluate their own welds honestly and identify defects accurately
The student maintains full PPE compliance during all welding and grinding operations without reminders
The student clears the welding area of flammable materials before welding and checks for smoldering materials afterward

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned what metal is. Now you learn what you can do with it. You are going to cut metal, weld metal, grind metal, and build things from raw stock. This is fabrication — the ability to take a piece of metal that is one shape and turn it into something useful that is another shape.

Fabrication is the trade that makes other trades possible. The welder builds the jigs that the woodworker uses. The fabricator makes the brackets that hold plumbing pipes, the gates that protect property, the frames that support machines, the trailers that haul equipment. When something metal breaks on a farm, a job site, or in a workshop, the person who can cut, fit, and weld it back together keeps everything moving. The person who cannot waits for someone else and pays for the privilege.

But metalworking at this level introduces serious hazards. An angle grinder spins a disc at 10,000 RPM and will remove flesh as efficiently as it removes a weld bead. A welding arc produces ultraviolet light intense enough to burn your eyes without you feeling it at the time — you feel it hours later when your eyes feel like they are full of sand. Sparks from cutting and grinding are hot enough to start fires and burn through clothing. Hot metal from welding looks exactly like cold metal, and you will not know the difference until you grab it.

Every safety precaution in this packet exists because someone was hurt when they did not follow it. You will respect these precautions not out of fear but out of understanding. The person who works metal safely for decades is not lucky — they are disciplined.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Metal cutting** — using a hacksaw and angle grinder to cut metal to dimension safely and accurately
2. **MIG welding fundamentals** — running beads, basic joint welding, and understanding what welding actually does metallurgically
3. **Grinding and finishing** — cleaning welds, removing burrs, and preparing surfaces
4. **Fabrication process** — measuring, marking, cutting, fitting, welding, and finishing as an integrated sequence
5. **Weld quality evaluation** — reading your own welds critically and knowing when to reject and redo your work
6. **Load awareness** — understanding that welded connections carry force and that a bad weld is a failure waiting to happen
7. **PPE discipline** — welding helmet, safety glasses, hearing protection, leather gloves, and appropriate clothing as non-negotiable standards
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Cutting Metal

Metal cutting introduces new risks and new tools. You will learn two methods: the hacksaw (hand power) and the angle grinder (electric power).

**The hacksaw:** A metal frame holding a thin, hard blade with fine teeth. Hacksaws cut on the push stroke — push forward with steady pressure, lift slightly on the return stroke.

- **Blade selection matters.** The rule: at least three teeth must be in contact with the material at all times. Thin material needs a fine blade (24 or 32 teeth per inch). Thick material uses a coarser blade (14 or 18 TPI). If fewer than three teeth engage, the teeth straddle the material and catch — the blade jams and the teeth break.
- **Secure the workpiece.** Clamp the metal in a vise or with clamps so it cannot move. Cutting with one hand while holding the workpiece with the other is how hacksaw injuries happen — the blade slips and your hand is in the path.
- **Let the saw work.** Long, smooth strokes using the full blade length. Pushing too hard overheats the blade and dulls the teeth. Let the blade teeth do the cutting.
- **Cut to the line.** Mark your cut line with soapstone or a metal-marking pencil. Cut on the waste side of the line — if you cut on the line, you lose the line's width from your piece. Check your measurement before you cut. You cannot add metal back.

**The angle grinder:** One of the most versatile and most dangerous tools in any shop. An angle grinder spins an abrasive or cutting disc at high speed. It cuts metal with a thin cutting disc, grinds metal with a thick grinding disc, and finishes metal with a flap disc.

- **Disc selection.** Cutting discs are thin (1/16" or less) and cut by slicing through the metal. Grinding discs are thick (1/4") and remove material by abrasion. Flap discs are layered sandpaper that blend and finish surfaces. Never use a cutting disc for grinding (it will shatter) or a grinding disc for cutting (it is too thick to cut efficiently).
- **Guard position.** The grinder guard must always be in place, positioned between the disc and your body. If the disc fails — shatters, catches, or breaks — the guard deflects the fragments. Removing the guard "for better visibility" is how people lose eyes and fingers.
- **Two-hand operation.** Always hold the grinder with both hands — one on the body, one on the side handle. A grinder that catches in the work will torque violently. If you are holding it with one hand, it wins and the spinning disc goes where you do not want it.
- **Spark direction.** Sparks and hot metal fly from the disc in the direction of rotation. Position yourself and the work so sparks fly into a safe area — not toward your body, not toward flammable materials, not toward other people.
- **Disc inspection.** Before mounting any disc, inspect it for cracks, chips, or damage. A damaged disc can shatter at full speed. Run the grinder for 30 seconds in a safe direction before using it — if the disc is going to fail, better it happens when you are clear of the path.

### Part 2: MIG Welding Fundamentals

Welding is the process of joining metals by melting them together. MIG (Metal Inert Gas) welding — also called GMAW (Gas Metal Arc Welding) — uses an electric arc to melt the base metal and a consumable wire electrode that feeds continuously into the joint, adding filler metal. A shielding gas (typically 75% argon, 25% CO2 for mild steel) flows over the weld area to protect the molten metal from contamination by air.

**What actually happens when you weld:** An electrical circuit is created between the welder, the wire, the workpiece, and the ground clamp. When the wire touches the workpiece, current flows and an arc forms — a sustained electrical spark of incredible intensity and heat (approximately 6,000-10,000 degrees Fahrenheit). The arc melts both the wire and the base metal surface. The molten metals mix together in the weld puddle. As the puddle cools, it solidifies into a metallurgical bond — the wire and the base metal have literally become one continuous piece of metal. This is not glue. This is fusion.

**Safety first — always:**
- **Helmet.** The welding arc produces intense ultraviolet light that will burn your corneas (arc eye/flash burn). You will not feel the burn immediately — it develops hours later, and it feels like sand or broken glass in your eyes. An auto-darkening welding helmet darkens to shade 10-13 when the arc strikes. Never strike an arc without your helmet down. Never look at someone else's arc without proper shade protection.
- **Skin coverage.** The UV light also burns skin — a welding sunburn on your forearms from rolling up your sleeves is painful and entirely preventable. Wear long sleeves (cotton or leather — never synthetic fabrics, which melt onto skin), welding gloves, and button your collar.
- **Fire prevention.** Sparks and spatter from welding are molten metal. They will ignite paper, cardboard, sawdust, oily rags, and many other common shop materials. Clear the welding area of all flammable materials. Have a fire extinguisher within reach — not across the shop, within reach. Check the area for smoldering materials after welding and again 30 minutes later (fires from welding sparks often start slowly).
- **Ventilation.** Welding produces fumes — vaporized metals and flux that are harmful to breathe. Weld in a ventilated area or use a fume extraction system. Never weld in an enclosed space without forced ventilation.
- **Ground clamp.** The ground clamp must be attached directly to the workpiece or to the metal table the workpiece sits on. A poor ground connection causes an erratic arc, poor weld quality, and potential electrical hazard.

**Running your first bead:** Before welding a joint, you practice running beads — straight lines of weld on flat plate.

1. Set up a piece of 1/4" steel plate on a clean metal table. Attach the ground clamp.
2. Set the welder to manufacturer-recommended settings for the wire size and material thickness. (A good starting point for .030" wire on 1/4" plate: 18-19 volts, wire speed 280-320 inches per minute.)
3. Position yourself comfortably. You must be stable — bracing your arms or hands against the table if possible. Welding requires steady, controlled movement.
4. Hold the gun at approximately 15-20 degrees from vertical, tilted in the direction you are traveling (push technique for MIG). The contact tip should be about 3/8" to 1/2" from the workpiece.
5. Lower your helmet. Pull the trigger. The arc strikes. You should hear a steady crackling or buzzing sound — like frying bacon. This sound tells you the arc is stable and the settings are in the right range.
6. Move the gun steadily along your intended line. Travel speed controls bead width and penetration — too fast and the bead is thin and narrow with poor penetration; too slow and the bead is wide, tall, and the heat builds up excessively.
7. Focus on the weld puddle — the small pool of molten metal directly behind the arc. The puddle tells you everything: its shape, its brightness, whether it is flowing smoothly or sputtering. Learn to read the puddle.

**Common beginner problems:**
- *Bird's nest* (wire tangles in the gun): usually caused by the wire being too tight against the workpiece when you pull the trigger. Keep the tip slightly away.
- *Porosity* (holes in the weld): usually caused by insufficient shielding gas (check gas flow, check for drafts) or contaminated base metal (oil, rust, paint — clean before welding).
- *Cold lap* (weld sits on top of the base metal without fusing): insufficient heat. Increase voltage or slow down travel speed.
- *Burn-through* (the arc melts completely through the base metal): too much heat for the material thickness. Decrease voltage, increase travel speed, or use thicker material for practice.

### Part 3: Basic Joint Welding

Once you can run a consistent bead on flat plate, you progress to welding joints — connecting two pieces of metal.

**Butt joint:** Two pieces of flat metal placed edge to edge. This is the simplest joint. The weld fills the gap between the pieces and fuses both edges together. For thin material, no gap is needed. For thicker material (over 1/8"), a small gap (about 1/16") allows better penetration.

**T-joint:** One piece of flat metal standing on edge against another flat piece, forming a T shape. The weld fills the inside corner (the fillet) between the two pieces. T-joints are the most common joint in fabrication — virtually every frame, bracket, and structure uses T-joints.

**Lap joint:** Two pieces of flat metal overlapping each other. The weld runs along the edge of the top piece where it meets the surface of the bottom piece. Lap joints are common for attaching gussets, patches, and reinforcements.

For each joint type, practice in flat position (horizontal table) until your welds are consistent and free of obvious defects. Your mentor will teach you to evaluate your welds using visual inspection criteria.

### Part 4: Grinding and Finishing

Raw welds have spatter (small balls of molten metal that stuck to the surface), proud beads (weld metal that stands above the surface of the base material), and sometimes slag or discoloration. Grinding and finishing clean the work to a professional appearance.

**Grinding disc:** Removes material aggressively. Use for removing significant excess weld metal, cleaning up bad welds before re-welding, and rough shaping. Keep the grinder moving — holding it in one spot gouges the metal.

**Flap disc:** A layered abrasive disc that removes material more gently than a grinding disc. Use for blending welds, smoothing surfaces, and preparing for paint or coating. Flap discs produce a much more uniform finish than grinding discs.

**Wire wheel:** A brush made of wire that mounts on the angle grinder. Removes rust, scale, and light spatter without removing base metal. Excellent for cleaning before welding and for final cleanup after grinding.

**The finish standard:** Spatter removed. Weld profile consistent. Sharp edges from grinding are smoothed. The piece looks intentional, not accidental. A well-finished fabrication project communicates competence. A poorly finished one communicates carelessness, regardless of how good the welds are underneath.

### Part 5: The Fabrication Process

Fabrication is not a single skill — it is a process: design, measure, mark, cut, fit, weld, grind, finish. Each step depends on the quality of the step before it.

1. **Design.** Decide what you are building. Sketch it. Include dimensions.
2. **Measure and mark.** Transfer the dimensions to your raw material. Use a tape measure, square, and soapstone. Mark clearly.
3. **Cut.** Cut each piece to dimension. Cut on the waste side of the line. Check the cut piece against the drawing.
4. **Fit.** Assemble the cut pieces in their final positions. Check that everything fits — gaps are consistent, angles are correct, dimensions match the drawing. If something does not fit, fix it now. It is much easier to recut a piece than to grind out a weld and start over.
5. **Tack.** Small tack welds hold the pieces in position for final welding. A tack weld is a short bead — just enough to hold. Place tacks at strategic points so the assembly stays square and in position. Check square and dimensions again after tacking.
6. **Weld.** Complete the final welds. Weld in a sequence that minimizes distortion — welding heats the metal unevenly, which causes it to warp. Alternating sides and welding in short segments reduces warping.
7. **Grind and finish.** Clean all welds, remove spatter, smooth edges, and prepare for coating.

### Part 6: Evaluating Your Own Welds

The most important skill in welding is the ability to look at your own work honestly and know whether it is acceptable.

A good MIG weld on mild steel looks like a stack of dimes laid slightly overlapping — consistent ripples with even width, consistent height, and straight tracking. The edges of the weld should show fusion with the base metal — you should see the weld metal flowing into the base metal surface, not sitting on top of it.

**Visual defects to look for:**
- *Porosity:* Holes or pits in the weld surface — indicates gas shielding problems or contamination
- *Undercut:* A groove melted into the base metal along the edge of the weld — indicates too much heat or too fast travel
- *Cold lap:* The weld edge does not fuse with the base metal — indicates insufficient heat
- *Inconsistent width:* The bead wanders wider and narrower — indicates unsteady hand or inconsistent travel speed
- *Excessive spatter:* Many small balls of metal around the weld — indicates voltage or wire speed problems, or contaminated metal

When you see a defect, do not leave it. Grind it out and re-weld. The standard you walk past is the standard you accept. A fabricator who tolerates poor welds on practice projects will tolerate poor welds on structural projects, and that is when people get hurt.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Cutting Accuracy
**Developing well:** The student cuts metal to dimension within 1/16" using both hacksaw and angle grinder. Cut lines are straight. The student secures the workpiece, selects the correct blade or disc, and maintains safe body position throughout.

**Developing slowly:** Cuts are significantly off the line, the student does not secure the workpiece, or they use the wrong disc type. The angle grinder is held with one hand or the guard is removed.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student marking their cut lines clearly? Are they cutting on the waste side of the line? Have they had enough practice with clamping before cutting?

### 2. MIG Welding Quality
**Developing well:** Beads are consistent in width and height. Joint welds show fusion at the edges (no cold lap). The weld surface is free of porosity. The student maintains a stable arc sound (steady crackling). Bead appearance improves session over session.

**Developing slowly:** Beads are erratic — varying width, height, and direction. Porosity is frequent. The student cannot maintain a consistent arc. Improvement between sessions is minimal.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the machine settings correct? (Wrong settings make good welding impossible.) Is the student's body position stable? (Unsteady position produces unsteady welds.) Is the base metal clean? (Dirty metal produces porosity regardless of technique.) Is the student practicing enough? (Welding is a motor skill that develops through repetition — 20 minutes once a week is insufficient.)

### 3. Self-Evaluation Accuracy
**Developing well:** The student accurately identifies defects in their own welds and can describe the likely cause. Their self-evaluation aligns closely with your evaluation. They reject their own substandard work and redo it without prompting.

**Developing slowly:** The student thinks all their welds are fine, or thinks all their welds are terrible — they cannot distinguish quality levels. They do not identify specific defects or understand causes.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you spent enough time teaching them what to look for? Do they have examples of good and bad welds to compare against? Have you been honest in your evaluations, or have you been too generous (teaching them that mediocre work is acceptable)?

### 4. PPE and Safety Compliance
**Developing well:** Full PPE every time, no exceptions, no reminders. Fire prevention area cleared before welding. Fire check after welding. Ground clamp properly placed. Flammable materials cleared.

**Developing slowly:** The student needs reminders about PPE, forgets to clear the area, or skips post-weld fire checks.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling perfect PPE compliance yourself? Have you explained the specific injury mechanisms (arc eye, UV burns, fire from spatter) in concrete terms? Has the student been shown photographs of welding injuries? (Effective deterrent when age-appropriate.)

### 5. Fabrication Projects
**Developing well:** The student's fabrication projects match their designs. Measurements are accurate. Cuts are to dimension. The assembly is square. Welds are consistent. The finished piece is functional and clean. The student can narrate their process and explain their decisions.

**Developing slowly:** Projects do not match designs. Measurements are significantly off. The assembly is not square. Welds vary widely in quality. The student cannot explain their process.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student planning before cutting? Have they practiced the fit-up (assembling without welding) before committing to welds? Have they checked square after tacking? Fabrication errors are almost always planning errors.
